subject: Your leave records are in a WhatsApp chat
preview: Payroll week arrives and nobody agrees how many days Ahmed actually took in March.
opening:
It's mid-May and Oman's public holiday calendar just handed a lot of companies a short week.

Short weeks are stress tests. Approvals pile up on the wrong days. Someone takes an unlogged day. The attendance sheet goes stale. And by the time payroll week hits, you're not running numbers — you're investigating.

That investigation is usually conducted inside a WhatsApp group. Which tells you everything.

pain.title: The WhatsApp leave system is costing you more than you think
pain.body:
Most HR managers in Oman are managing leave across at least one group chat — sometimes three. Requests arrive between forwarded videos and birthday messages. Someone screenshots it. Someone approves verbally and logs nothing. Then month-end arrives and no one can reconstruct what actually happened.

The time cost alone is significant: HR teams at companies our size typically spend 6 to 10 hours a month just reconciling leave before they can close payroll. That's before you factor in the dispute with the employee, the wrong WPS figure, or the Ministry of Labour inspection where you have to explain why your leave records are a screenshot from March. SmartPro exists precisely for this — leave requests, approvals, balances, and payroll all talking to each other in one place.

proof.title: From 14 hours of payroll reconciliation to 23 minutes
proof.body: A 74-person retail business in Muscat came to SmartPro after three consecutive months of salary disputes — wrong overtime, mismatched leave balances, late WPS submissions. Their HR manager was spending 14 hours every month just reconciling payroll, and still getting figures wrong. After moving to SmartPro, their first fully automated payroll run took 23 minutes. One HR manager. Zero bank rejections. Attendance pulled directly from the system, approvals completed inside the platform, WPS file generated and submitted without touching a spreadsheet.

playbook.title: How to audit your leave process this week — before month-end hits
steps: Open your main HR WhatsApp group right now and count how many leave requests were sent in April that have no logged approval anywhere outside the chat. That number is your exposure.

Pull your current leave balance sheet — whatever form it lives in — and pick five employees at random. Ask each of them what their remaining balance is. If more than two give you a different number than your sheet, your data has already drifted.
Check whether your attendance record and your leave log are reconciled for May so far. If they live in separate files or separate systems, reconcile them today — not the night before payroll closes.

Identify the one person who currently owns your leave data. Ask yourself what happens to payroll week if they're sick for three days. If the answer is 'crisis,' document the process this week while it's fresh.

Before your next payroll run, set a hard rule: no leave is considered approved unless it exists in writing outside WhatsApp — email, a form, a system entry. Tell your team this week, not after the next dispute.

If you're still running this on spreadsheets, calculate the actual hours your HR person spends on leave reconciliation per month. Write that number down. It's the starting point for any honest conversation about whether the current setup is worth keeping.

question: When a Ministry of Labour inspector walked in tomorrow morning, how long would it take your team to pull a complete, accurate leave record for any one employee — and what would you have to open to do it?
signoff:
If any step in that playbook made you uncomfortable, that's useful information.

The companies that handle compliance calmly aren't doing more work — they're doing it in one place instead of five.
